JUNE. 
The breath of June is in the air, 
And that exquisite fragrance rare, 
That thrills the soul with glad delight, 
And over all the verdant land, 
Beauty hath waved her magic wand. 
And earth is bathed in radiant light. 

The forest songsters tune their lays, 
And thro' the woodland, wilding ways, 
Or fields and meadows, gay with flowr’s, 
Their melody in chorus sweet,
Makes earth seem fairly-land complete, 
A foretaste of fair Eden’s bow’rs.
The June of life again we feel,
And softly o’er our senses steal, 
The calmness, and the sweet repose. 
Rejuvenating all our powers, 
And the glad muse of summer hours,
Within the heart of sunshine glows. 

The sunsets’ blaze of colors bright
Pales to an amethystine light, 
As gently falls the evening dew, 
And mellow moonlight softly sheds,
Its healing balm on aching heads
And stars bespangle heavn’s pale blue. 

Such respite from life’s toil and woe,
Is given us, while here below
As visions, of the world unseen;
To stimulate our lives to bear, 
The ills and griefs, we needs must share, 
Until we reach the heights serene. 

O, how we long to climb those hills,
And quaff from out the sparkling rills, 
The honeyed nectar of our youth;
For there it flows in limpid streams, 
Of which our fancy only dreams, 
Springing from wells of living truth. 

O nights of June! Fairer than all!
From out the silence voices call, 
In echoes from the fountains deep, 
Or from the far-off woods and vales, 
Or distant seas, whose mournful wails, 
Tell where the dead, unnumber’d sleep.
June, 1896 E. B. W.


PARIS, May 29.—M. Gaston Bruno
Paulin Paris, the French philologist, has
 been elected to the seat in the French
academy rendered vacant, by the death of
Alexander Dumas.


A CONGRATULATION.

[To my venerated friends Brother and Sister
Horne on the sixtieth anniversary of their
Wedding Day.
EMILY H. WOODMANSEE.
Happy their fate, who truly mate, 
	Who wear the yoke, that love makes light,
Who joyfully can celebrate
	Their marriage day, and count it bright;
But few are favor’d thus to see—
Its sixtieth anniversary.

Exactly, one decade ago,
	Upon your golden wedding day;
Your friends, a happy overflow,
	The old and young, the grave and gay
Met ’neath your sheltering roof—to share
The joy of that occasion rare.

Since then, a host of friends have died,
	And many strange events we’ve seen,
Unlooked for woes have multiplied,
	And life an uphill road has been,
And every one must needs confess,
All things are shaken, more or less.

Whether or no, ’tis so designed
	As Saints believe, and scoffers doubt,
Amid the general strife we find
	That something good, has been wrought out,
And Zion! Once “the trodden down”
	Is gaining favor and renown.

Now, unto Zion, are secured
	Great Rights, withheld, in days gone by—
No longer friendless and obscur’d,
	But like a "lily set on high” 
Or like a bright attractive star,
	Lo! Zion’s light, shines forth afar.

So visible to eyes unshut
	Is God’s own marvelous enterprise;
“The Stone” from out the mountain cut,
	Is rolling on and gaining size,
A Nucleus, gath’ring power apace,
	To fill the earth, and bless the race.

And you my friends, who from the first,
	Dared to espouse the cause of Truth?
Who many times have braved the worst,
	In Truth’s defense, are glad forsooth
To know that come, whatever, may—
	Established Truth, on earth will stay.

Especially you can rejoice,
	That sons and daughters ’round you throng.
That each and all with heart and voice,
	Are helping Zion’s cause along,
Truth’s representatives are they,
Who bear your honored name today.

Albeit, that you’re greatly blest
	Perchance, like other folk you’re tried,
Earth is not yet a place of rest,
	Nor are we all yet sanctified;
And trouble, bolder than before,
Walks in, at nearly every door.

Yet side by side, in woe or weal, 
	Linked hand and heart, along you go,
As happily united still,
	As in youth’s early morning glow
And may you hence enjoy—I pray—
An everlasting wedding day.
May 9, 1896.


UTAH FEDERATION OF WOMAN’S
CLUBS.

	THE third annual convention of the Utah
Federation of Women’s Clubs was held in
Unity Hall in this City, Wednesday and
Thursday May 20 and 21, meeting com-
mencing at 10 a. m. and at 2 p.m. of each
day. President Emma J. McVicker pre-
sided, Miss Emma McCornick acted as
secretary. Wednesday morning was de-
voted exclusively to business. The Presi-
dent appointed a credential committee.
Mesdames Green of Provo, Bannister of
Ogden and La Barthe of this City. 
	Committee on organization Mrs. E.
B. Wells chairman, reported one
club organized during the past year. The
Federation elected Mrs. Corinne M. Allen
as its representative at the annual meeting
of the General Federation at Louisville. 
REAPERS’ CLUB.
	REPORT of the Reapers’ Club read at the
Stake Federation of Clubs, Salt Lake City, 
May 20th, 1896. By Romania B. Pratt.
The Reapers Club is now in its fourth year
of useful instruction, with a membership
roll of forty-three, and an average atten-
dance of twenty-five. It convenes every
two weeks at the WOMAN’S EXPONENT
parlors, on Monday afternoon. The course
of study is Miscellany, Literature, Political 
Science and Current Events. The club
color is red and the badge is a wheat head. 
	The President and Secretary retire at
each meeting inviting two members to fill
these positions, to promote general education
in presiding and taking notes. Under the
head of Current Literature there have been
written and commented upon about twenty
sketches of the lives and works of noted
novelists, poets, philosophers, divines
theorists, musicians and artists. 
	The later sessions have been devoted to
the Study of Political Science followed by
questions and discussion. 
	The object of this club is social and in-
tellectual development. Its design is to
cultivate the heart as well as the brain
which will form a good mental equilibrium
and fit woman not only to shine as a greater
light around her own hearthstone, but to be
a more efficient custodian of home interests
in the wider domain of the world. 
	Progress is improvement upon the past;
and the study of the lives and works of
eminent men and women who have lived
before us or are contemporary, stimulates
to emulation. 
	The club women of Utah are the high
priestessess at the altar of the home, often
consecrated to plain living but also to high
thinking, where the noblest ideals are cher-
ished and where money is not King. 
	The Reapers Club of busy and earnest
women is as a small streamlet singing a
glad song of pride and thankfulness as it
glides into the larger stream of the State
Federation, and with it sweeps into the
General Federation of Woman’s Clubs,
helping to create a mighty force of womanly
power which will raise the standard of mor-


[Column 1]
als in the world and spiritualize and refine
the material and physical in man and thus
hasten the era of peace on earth and good
will to all men.
	The women of the Reapers’ Club do not
intend to be gleaners only from the good
works of others, but hope through industry,
to leave to posterity a legacy of their own
noble thoughts and improved methods of
life, that it may truly be said of them that
"the world is better for their having lived
in it.” 
	The women of the Reapers Club aim to
grasp the sickle of industry and enter the
fields of science and knowledge to reap and
bind into sheaves, golden truths, with
which to store the granaries of the intellect
as food for thought and action in a daily
progressive life, in all that is helpful and
uplifting to the human race.
	Mrs. J. M. Kimball, of Ogden, reported
La Coterie, of which we have no synopsis.
Mrs. Elmer B. Jones of Salt Lake and
	Mrs. E. B. Graham of Ogden were nomi-
nated as the first and second choice for state
correspondents of the General Federation
	Mrs. W. C. Jennings was elected Utah
Correspondent for the Lotus, the official
organ of Women’s Clubs.
	The Author’s Club of Salt Lake was ad-
mitted to membership, its delegate in the
convention was Augusta W. Grant. This
closed the business meeting.
	Tempting lunch was served in the par-
lors of the hall by Salt Lake club women;
all the visiting delegates were entertained
as also all the Presidents and delegates of
the City Clubs.
	The afternoon session opened with an ad-
dress of welcome by Mrs. E. La Barthe
who extended to all a cordial welcome.
	Madame President, Delegates to the Fed-
eration, Visitors from Neighboring Cities,
Friends:—It is my pleasure in behalf of the
home clubs to bid you welcome—welcome
to the metropolis of Utah—Salt Lake City.
	Welcome to the capital of this young
State, within whose domain assembled not
long since a memorable convention, the
record of which shall go down to posterity
as having granted the privilege of suffrage
to women, and which State, it is hoped,
with our help will soon enter upon a new
career of purer politics and better govern-
ment.
	Welcome to the birthplace of the first wo-
man’s club of the far West. In this beauti-
ful valley, nearly a score of years ago was
organized the incipiency of today’s auspici-
ous gathering, many of the devoted mem-
bers of which unite in sending you greeting.
	Welcome to the cause for which you have
come. The State Federation stands a con-
necting link between individual clubs and
the General Federation. New clubs are con-
stantly coming into existence. In some
States the increase is phenomenal. Utah is
doing its share. They are organized on an
independent basis; arrange a plan of work
and bring results to the State gathering.
The practical interests of women are multi-
plying so rapidly that only in this way can
we follow them and give their value to the
world.
	Welcome to this Federation, which is
paramount in importance to any preceding
one, because it is the first and largest as-
semblage of women in the new State. It
should establish our ability to perform a
part more vital and more dignified in the
large affairs of  our time; it should secure

[Column 2]
us leadership in matters now pressing for
action in our State. We represent the wo-
men of Utah, of diverse ages, tastes and
faith, banded together by common interest
in all that pertains to the advancement and
progress of women. Greeting to you is
universal. Moroni from the Temple’s
highest pinnacle trumpets it; the Blind
Goddess surmounting our city and county’s
pride justifies it; the eagle from our city
gate screams it; the flag from our Govern-
ment building, our club home on Fifth
East flaunts it; the signal guns at Fort
Douglas boom your welcome. All nature
is in unison; the birds sing it, the blossoms,
flowers and foliage lend their incense; the
wind whispers it, while we proclaim it is a
joy to have you with us; we are proud to
do you honor; the proceedings to follow
shall be inspired by your presence; we
esteem it a privilege to proffer our hos-
pitality. Again, we bid you sincere and
heartfelt welcome.
	In reply to Mrs. La Barthe’s cordiality,
Mrs. McVicker, as president of the Federa-
tion, said:
	Ladies of the Salt Lake Clubs; In behalf
of the Utah Federation of Women’s Clubs
I respond to your cordial welcome. I assure
you that we appreciate most deeply your
care and labor in providing for our comfort
and enjoyment while here, but especially do
we feel your generous words of encourage-
ment and appreciation of our great work of
advancing the intellectual, social and moral
development of the women of Utah.
	May we attain to the high standard you
have outlined for us, and our efforts be felt
for good in all that concerns our new State.
	It is said that all good things come by
threes. It is certain that the triad in music
is the foundation of all harmony. In the
triad of club organization our State Federa-
tion represents the mediant, the individual
club being the tonic and the general Federa-
tion the dominant. As there can be no
harmony without the gentle mediant, lying
between the strong foundation tone and the
imperial fifth, so may the State Federation
fill its appropriate mission of bringing the
individual clubs into accord with the great
dominant Federation. Thus shall the full
harmony swell and roll until it floods all
parts of our land.
DR. ELLIS R. SHIPP.
	Ladies and Gentlemen: I have the
honor of representing the Utah Woman’s
Press Club. Since making our last report
to this noted organization, we have made
most gratifying progress, having admitted
during the past year many new members
all bright, active workers, ladies of high
literary taste and talents.
	We do not limit our membership, one of
the primary objects of our organization be-
ing to encourage young writers and culti-
vate all their literary possibilities, our hon-
ored president being an adept in bringing
out latent talent. Although many of the
results would not perhaps shine with
Shakespearean brilliancy yet we have
listened to many choice thoughts that
would otherwise never had birth. Our
motto is “The most good to the greatest
number." Our membership not being con-
fined to the environs of Salt Lake City
only, but extending through the domain of
the State of Utah.
	In reviewing our past year’s work one is
surprised with the amount of original work
done by this club, the greater part of every 

[Column 3]
session being devoted to this line of work.
	Our subjects for consideration and dis-
cussion have been historical, biographical,
scientific, poetical and polit cal. We have
debated the “Double money standard for
America,” the “Single tax” question, and
have recently formulated a plan for the
study of Parliamentary Law.
	Our meetings are held but once a month
unless called by the President for the
transaction of business.
	According to our by-laws we elect officers
once a year, no one is permitted to hold an
office longer than two successive years, thus
giving the greater number the opportunity
for the experience.
	During the past year it has been our
privilege to entertain a number of notable
people. In August last the gentlemen of
the local press courteously invited the Wo-
man’s Press Club to assist in entertaining
the Convention of Western editors then
enroute for the Pacific Coast. The Misses
Chase of Massachusetts gave us a glowing
description of their foreign travels in various
parts of the world; also Dr. Sarah J. Elliot
who awakened in our minds many
new ideas by her life-drawn pictures of the
noble self-sacrificing labors of Miss Clara
Barton. Mrs. Laura M. Johns the suffrage
speaker and organizer was also entertained
by us quite recently, and last though far
from least—the officers of the Utah Federa-
tion of Woman’s Clubs, by special invita-
tion honored us with their presence.
	Frequently is our club entertained most
bounteously at the homes of its members,
thus fostering a spirit of sociability which
in our case is the main-spring of love and
unity, this in turn being the true secret of
strength and power.
	In closing we would express as the senti-
ment of the Utah Woman’s Press Club:
The press—the friend of justice and reason,
the best exponent of virtue and a potent
subduer of vice—the promoter of intelli-
gence, knowledge and education. As
Phillips has said: “From the hour printing
was invented the brain and not the arm,
the thinker and not the soldier, books and
not kings, were to rule the world. Weap-
ons forged in the mind, keen-edged and
brighter than the sunbeam were to supplant
the sword and the battleax.”


WOMAN S EXPONENT


MINUTES of meeting held in WOMAN'S
EXPONENT Office, May 19, 1896, Mrs.
Kate Groesbeck in the chair. Sang, “Help
the working women.” Prayer by Mrs.
Oswald. Sang’ “Columbia’s daughters.”
Minutes of last meeting read and approved;
after which the secretary read a lecture on
townships from Kirke’s Civil Government.
The president made some remark on Civil
Government and spoke about holding parlor
meetings, thought that the ladies could ex-
press their views better in an informal meet-
ing than they could in the regular meeting.
Mrs. J. Fewson Smith made some remarks
on common law. Mrs. Grames read from
Roberts rules of order. C. Newman
read the Constitution of W. S. A. of Utah
and also read the article from the Hartford
daily times on “Universal Suffrage” by
Elizabeth Burrill Curtis. The President
favored taking the study of Woman’s
Manual of Parliamentary Law. A parlor
meeting was appointed to be held two
weeks from date at Mrs. Newman’s home.
Adjourned for one month. Benediction by
Mrs. Holling.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT


THE SPRING OF 1896.
WHEN it is evening, ye say: It will be fair weather
	For the sky is red,
And in the morning, it will be foul weather
	Today, for the sky is red and lowering.

March did not come in like a gentle lamb;
	And it left like a roaring lion;
But we expected storms for ’tis well known
	March is not a month to rely on,

But we thought perchance that April,
	The month for shine and showers,
Would gladden earth with warmth enough
	To bring forth waiting flowers.

But we never thought that May time
	Would bring us frost and snow;
For surely if we harvest
	We must have time to sow.

The sun has gone down in splendor,
	The morning brought forth rain,
The clouds have hung low in the morning,
	Ere night we had sunshine again.

The bow of the moon would hold the horn, 
	Yet sunshine has come with the rain;
So what e’er our faith in by-gone years
	We cannot trust the moon again.

The Snipe’s sad cry more rain, more rain
	Has been answered with Meadowlarks thrill;
And in spite of frost, hail and snow,
	The grass and wee blossoms still

Have been growing; and green is showing
	All through the furrowed field:
And yester the bow of promise
	Gave hope for a harvest yield.

We had better be up and doing;
	“Improve the shining hours”
If God wills that we must have rain
	We should work between the showers.
						M. E. T. Griffeth. 
Fairview, Idaho.


[Column 1]
LEAVES FROM MY JOURNAL. 
	PARIS, Oct. 13, 1895. A merry party
left Hotel Central for the depot, taking the
first class “Raapide” at 9 a. m. for
Marsailles, going via Dijon, Macon and
Lyons. The train was most comfortable
with conveniences such as we have at home,
but divided into compartments, our party
had two, we had our meals aboard, lunch
and dinner being excellent. We drank
mineral water which I did not like. We
passed some beautiful scenery and saw
many vineyards. Dijon is now the centre
of trade for the wines of Upper Burgundy.
Arrived just at nightfall at Lyons, the
capital of the department, of the Rhone,
situated on the tongue of land, inclosed
between the Rhone and the Saone. We
saw from the windows the lighted city,
the Church of Notre Dame de Tourvieres.
A square which we supposed was that of
Louis le Grand, and the quays along the
Rhone, the lamps were numerous so we
could see very well, and regretted not stop-
ping a day to visit the manufactories and
the Palais de Beaux Arts which contains
interesting Roman Antiquities. 
	At eleven p. m. we arrived at Marsailles
the city was “en fete” the scene very gay
the square was beautiful with lights, flowers
tropical plants and fruits, the people were
singing and laughing and serving refresh-
ments. The soldiers were preparing to sail
for Algiers, on the morrow, and seemed as
happy as if going on a pleasure trip.
	We drove to Grand Hotel de Noailles,
the entrance to court is beautiful with

[Column 2]
palms and potted plants, and as we always
find in France, the hotel is most comfortable
the beds and rooms delightfully clean; it is
a pleasure to rest in them. Much fatigued
we all retired. 
	Friday, 14th Oct. Directly after we had
finished our breakfast, Mr. Mc V. ordered
carriages and we drove about the city.
Marsailles, is an ancient city being founded
by the Phoceians 600 B. C., in S. France,
the capital of the department of the months
of the Rhone—on the Meditteranean, and 
on the east coast of the Gulf of Lyons, en-
closed by a succession of rocky hills extend-
ing in the form of a crescent, until each
extremity reaches the sea. The form of
the city is nearly square; it is surrounded by
walls and separated into the Old and New
town by “one of the finest streets in
Europe.” The Old town has mean and
dirty streets—the New, is beautiful and
much like Paris, the Hotel de Ville, is an
architectural beauty—the Ancient Cathedral
is Gothic and said to have been built on the
ruins of a temple of Diana. The public
library is large and there are cabinets of
natural history, botanical gardens, and an
observatory, the Arch erected by Nap. I.
is very beautiful and much like those of
Paris, the Water Castle and fountain is of
extreme beauty and must be seen to be ap-
preciated. Next we drove to the water, the
Port of Marsailles, is called a Salt Water
Lake, and is completely sheltered from all
winds. The entrance does not exceed a
hundred yards, being confined by two pro-
jecting rocks, one on each side; on which
are situated two forts—for its defence, it is
protected by other works and completely
surrounded by quays; in driving on the
street fronting the entrance our guide told
us, when the mistrel wind blows, 'twas
said a battalion of men standing two
abreast could not stand against it; from
here we had our first peep at the Meditter-
ranean, and a magnificent view of the Bay
and Islands—the water was beautifully
blue and not a puff of wind to tell us of the
“Mistral.” On the hill to the south of the
Harbor, is a place of pilgrimage, The
old church has been replaced by a modern
building, Byzantine style, on the facade is
a belfry one hundred and fifty feet high,
surmounted by a statue of the Virgin, one
hundred and forty steps leads to the lower
church, one hundred and seventy four to
the upper—the view repays one for the
climb—the view at the foot is magnificent. 
The Church of St. Victor is beautiful and
in front is a bronze statue “by Reams,”
of M. de Belyunce, in prayer. The
Marsailles good bishop, celebrated by
Pope,” who during the plague 1720 re-
mained performing the solemn duties of his
office. At noon we left this most interest-
ing city with a wish to see it again.
	Taking the train for Cannes the journey
was short and interesting, we had exquisite
bits of landscapes and the Islands and bits
of water views equally as beautiful—while
passing the stations there were pretty little 
flower gardens and well dressed people wait-
ing for the trains; the air was perfumed but
all was not clover—we saw many and
varied scenes, not so entrancing. Soon we
reached our station and climbing  into a
dillegance, we drove up, up the hill each
turn revealing more beautiful views of
villas, landscape, gardens, flower spread
banks, and the “Sea—reflecting the azure
of a matchless sky,” the party exhausted,
the adjectives, ere we reached, Hotel de

[stamped below the second column]
HISTORIAN'S OFFICE LIBRARY
The Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints
23963

[Column 3]
Prince of Wales. When we smiled to hear
Mr. B. exclaim, “O, beautiful Dublin, you
are eclipsed by Cannes, with the roses, the
palms, the oranges and lemons the superb
views, the beautiful Bay, let us go home
Mollie while we can carry with us the
beauty of the scene, the fragrance of the
flowers and the music of the birds, it will
all be obliterated by musty tombs, when we
reach Rome.” The hotel was on the slope
of a hill, nestled in flowers and tropical
fruits, the palms the most perfect I ever
saw. Cannes is beautifully situated on the
Bay of the same name; from my window
the view of City and Bay was delightful,
we had perfect weather and flowers and
fruit in abundance; this is a great resort for 
invalids, but it seemed to me the perfume
was oppressive and it was too warm for
comfort. Many wealthy people have
beautiful residences here to avoid the rigors
of winter. 
	’Twas here Bonaparte landed on 1st of
March 1815, on his return from Elba. 
	The promenades are delightful, with the
orange palm, and pepper trees lining the
walk. 
	At night some musicians came and sang
delightfully among them, one song heard
iu Venice, “Jammo, Jammo, Ncoppa
Jammo, ja,” and is the one the Italians sing
as they ascend Mt. Vesuvius.
							KIM.


ADDRESS TO RELIEF SOCIETY.

	To the Relief Society of Centerville. I
ask you to pardon me if I may appear to be
obtrusive in the liberty I have taken, by ad-
dressing you in this manner. While at the
Relief Society union today I was impressed
with a very pleasant feeling of pleasure,
which is characteristic of this organization.
It was not only a repetition of what I al-
ways enjoy at these meetings, but while
there I was led to reflect upon the object of
the Relief Society and what it has done, I
think language will fail me, but still
enough can be said, to encourage the sisters
in that noble work. In the language of
scripture, to feed the hungry, clothe the
naked, to nurse and administer to the sick
embraces the highest point of the moral
law that can be expected of humanity; the
fact of which, is so well acknowledged by
all sects, all creeds, and all people whether
Christian or barbarian; and woman, with
her feminine nature and her sympathetic
feelings has so well adapted her for the
position that she has done her work so well
that it really appears to be one of the great
est essentials to human happiness. She has
labored for 6000 years in the good work
and no wonder that the Prophet Joseph
Smith was inspired to organize the Relief
Society, as he saw how much more good
could be done in a united capacity; may
the organization always have that support
that they deserve, and now the franchise
has been given her, it is to be hoped her
new obligations may not lessen her useful
ness in her acts of mercy nor burden her
with life’s cares, that she may not share in
all that pertains to human happiness. I be-
lieve with suffrage, woman will be able to
extend her usefulness in helping to improve
the civil affairs of society in other ways,
beside relieving the needy, and so far as
patronage in civil government she will find
every place for which she i[-] by nature and
qualification adapted.
JOHN WAYMAN.

THE WORLD IN COMMOTION.

	THE world at large was never in greater
commotion than at the present time; even
although desolating wars are not raging so
extensively as at some periods, yet there
are wars of various kinds, of the elements
of earth and air, and of men’s unbridled
passions fearful and terrible calamities,
cyclones, floods, disasters by sea, and on
land, until the very atmosphere seems
freighted heavily with destructive forces of
many kinds. In view of all these things 
one is forced to contemplate causes, and to
look forward to results; to contrast that
which exists today with the history of the
past, and compare it with the outlook for
the future of the world. Verily knowledge
increaseth and wisdom hideth itself, for
“Knowledge is proud that he has learned so much
Wisdom is humble that he knows no more.”
	Looking at the events transpiring around
the world today, one must necessarily feel
that with all the boasted knowledge of this
century of civilization, the hidden paths of
wisdom are obscure, and only found by a
few, who look upward for light and search
after eternal truth, as the prophets of old
and who were despised and scoffed at, but
whose words were revered by later genera-
tions, and if one would profit by such les-
sons he must beseech the Lord in all
humility for guidance.
	The signs of the times warn those who
have studied the Scriptures and believe in
the fulfillment of prophecy not only that
“in the last days perilous times shall come”
but that such perilous times have already
come, and such wide spread destruction
that men’s hearts quake for fear, and it is a
vexation to read of these things, and how
much more so when they are where one
must needs see, and feel them. It certainly
does not seem to be a time of rejoicing,
when notwithstanding the efforts made by
philanthropists and reformers to create an
era of peace, the facts prove that “there is
no peace,” because of the wickedness that
stalks broadcast and must of necessity pro-
voke the vengeance of the Almighty, who
delights not in unrighteousness, and who
will not be mocked.
	The terrible cyclone in St. Louis, the
terrific tornadoes in Texas, in Ohio and in
Kansas, the disastrous fire in Philadelphia
which incurred loss of life as well as
property, the burning of Cripple Creek,
Colorado all these fatalities seem near
enough to strike home to the hearts of all
who are interested in current events. The


political disturbances which have been fer-
menting, and likely to culminate in the dis-
ruption of the old parties, one cannot help
deploring, even though Utah the new star
in the horizon of the Western empire may
remain secure and undisturbed; surely one
cannot help realizing she has come into her
proud estate on the eve of dangerous times
to the nation, of which her people have been
so proud. But the hopeful should look for
a happy outcome from this agitation and
commotion. The women of Utah who
have just come into their political rights,
should be calm and dignified in the midst
of the tumult of ideas and opinions, and ex-
ercise such prudence and caution in times of
disorder and confusion, as will entitle them
to respect at home and admiration from all
who desire to see order come out of chaos.


[Column 1]
EDITORIAL NOTES.

	THE poem by Lydia D. Alder entitled,
“Mormon Tabernacle Choir in California,”
“A story in verse” is a recital of the visit
of the choir to the Pacific Coast, and very
appropriately dedicated to Professor Evan
Stephens, their leader. It is a booklet of
sixteen pages, fine clear type and very good
paper. A colored paper cover tied
with ribbon, gives it the appearance of what
it really is, a souvenir of the Choir’s visit to
the land of the golden gate. The poem is
divided under several sub-headings THE
JOURNEY, SAN FRANCISCO, DEL MONTE,
MONTEREY, HOMEWARD. It is quite unique
in delineation and very smoothly written,
one or two quotations break the rhythm of
the composition, but these were necessary
to make it correct in detail. The choir
should acknowledge the merit of the work,
and each have a copy as it is a historical poem
of their entire trip, and will thus be pre-
served much better than a newspaper article.
The booklet is for sale at the office of the
Juvenile Instructor and at this office, 10 cts.
per copy.
	THE First Commencement Recital of the
Utah School of Elocution was held at the
Social Hall on Friday evening June 12, 1896.
The graduates were Rose Anna Cannon,
Salt Lake City, and Anna Elizabeth Spencer,
Draper. The opening musical exercise
violin and piano duet by Katharine Rom-
ney and Dorothy Cannon was very effective.
Miss R. A. Cannon’s rendering of the
“Monks Magnificat” a brilliant success.
“Molly” by Miss Spencer and “An Old
Sweetheart of Mine” by Miss Cannon, were
very pleasing; but that which gave the best
idea of their excellent work was “The
Apple of Life” by Miss Spencer and Ten-
nyson’s “Maud” by Miss Cannon. Other
selections were successfully rendered and
the entire program was well carried out;
that the young ladies were beautiful and the
stage artistically decorated and managed it
is needless to say. Miss Babcock made a-
few remarks and explanations at the close,
and in a very graceful manner gave the
finishing touch to the entertainment by
awarding the certificates.
	THE closing school exercises during the
month of June have been more than usually
interesting, and the public schools of the
City have been written up in the daily
papers elaborately. The schools in the
Country districts compare very favorably
with those in the City. In Farmer’s Ward
the average attainments were high, and the
exhibition given in the evening was cer-

[Column 2]
tainly worthy of mention. The operatta
chosen was “Red Ridinghood” and the
rendering reflected great credit upon both
teachers and children. The singing was
specially fine in some parts and  the little
“tots” carried out their part of the program
wonderfully well. The recitations were
very creditable, Master Geo. Q. Cannon
gave a Military poem, and Master Eugene
Sears “Life’s Game of Ball.” Mr. Short, Miss
Burnett, Miss Wade and other teachers
deserve great credit for their careful train-
ing of the little ones in their parts to bring
them out so successfully.
	THE article by W. S. Harwood in the
May number of the North American Re-
view is being quoted by the newspapers of the
day and commented upon, as against women
knowing their true positton and standing
according to the XIV amendment to the
Constitution. Now the facts are that wo-
men throughout the country knew how the
law stood and tested it to its full extent.
Miss Anthony tried the fourteenth amend-
ment thoroughly, even to imprisonment for
attempting to vote. The Supreme court of
the United States decided this point long
ago, and from that court there is no other
appeal. This subject has come up in every
Convention which has been held in Wash-
ington for a number of years, and it is very
amusing that one of the greatest magazines
in America should publish the article. As
to women’s votes being counted out when
voting for a President the fact has been
proven, and a precedent established when
the women voted in the state of Wyoming.


FANNY’S BABY.

FANNY'S babe is sweetly sleeping
Cease O, cease your tears and weeping,
His spirit’s in the angels keeping
	Up among the stars!
Where he waits to see your coming
	Peeping through the golden bars.

Baby’s resting from his playing
	Softly in his bed he’s laying,
And the angel’s voice is saying
	“Softly sleep, softly sleep,”
Here the roses all are blooming
	Some for the baby we will keep!

Hark! The angel’s faintly singing
On the air the notes are ringing—
Up and down they now are bringing
	Garlands for the baby’s brow.
“Softly sleep, softly sleep,”
	Baby’s with the angels now.

Closed his eyes where love was beaming
And the happy smile was gleaming
Earthly joy is only seeming
	But the rest is sure and sweet,
Baby’s happy, baby’s singing
	Baby and his Grandma meet.

Beauteous blossoms by the Reaper,
Oft are gathered, like the sleeper;
Taken home where love is deeper
	Sheltered safe from every storm.
Choicest flowers are early gathered
	Placed where south winds, blow so warm.

Darling Fanny, safe’s your treasure,
	Where love abounds without a measure,
And life flows on with heavenly pleasure,
	Where rarest joys are now complete.
Lift your heart and eyes to Heaven,
	List! Your baby coo’s so sweet.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT


There the links that here are broken
Are joined in Heaven, and by this token
We know that Christ to us hath spoken
	‘‘Let the little ones, come to me”
Up among the shining mansions
	In God’s kingdom they will be.
					LYDIA D. ALDER.
	Sunday, June 7th, 1896.


[Column 1]
LADIES’ SEMI-MONTHLY MEETING.

	MINUTES of meeting held in the Four-
teenth Ward Assembly Hall April 18, 1896.
President M. Isabella Horne presiding.
Singing “Come come ye Saints.” Prayer
by Margaret F. Steele. Neither of the
secretaries being present Julia C. Howe
read the minutes of previous meeting which
were approved and Mary L. Morris acted
as secretary. 
	President Horne addressed the sisters;
spoke of having been absent from the
meetings some weeks on account of sick-
ness in the family, desired to fill her mis-
sion as a teacher in Israel, wished to be
humble and bear her part acceptably before
God, urged the sisters to be very watchful,
“Satan does not sleep, we are having an
easy time and we must be the more diligent,
easiness is dangerous; there are trials ahead
both for men and women, but the spirit of
God will guide us aright, we are to be tried
in all things, the time is not far distant 
when the dividing line will come, trials are
friends in disguise, let us be careful lest
after we have taught others, we may be a
castaway. Just as soon as we become lifted
up we are in great danger. I have lived to
see Counselors of the Prophet Joseph re-
moved, let us not speak against the Lord’s
anointed, whatever we think, let us not
speak it;” spoke of lying reports and lying
spirits, referred to the Lord wanting to
punish Ahab; also spoke of Job and a coun-
cil held in heaven and of Satan wanting to 
save people in their sins.
	Mary L. Morris followed bearing testi-
mony and speaking of the Gospel being
free to all, but they must come in at
the door, as we had done.
	E. J. Stevenson spoke of the sacred
minutes taken by the secretary Eliza R.
Snow, when the Prophet organized the
Relief Society, remembered Sister Horne’s
teaching when she carried her babies in her
arms, and could never, no, never forget it,
asked the sisters to pray for Sister Horne
and Sister Zina and cherish the sweet in-
fluence they felt in that house.
	Sister Francis, “let us be one, if we are
not united, the evil one will creep in among
us, made other good remarks. Sister Burns,
“let us honor the priesthood, I love to read
the Bible, my mother taught me to read a
little every day, and I found that the min-
isters of the sects of the day could not
answer my questions. I read the book of 
Mormon on my knees and asked God to
show me if it was true, he answered my
prayer. Miss Glade testified that she had
been a witness that the dead had been
raised, urged the sisters to keep the Word
of Wisdom strictly. Agnes Hunter testi-
fied to what Miss Glade had said, also said
that as soon as she received the Gospel she
had the spirit of gathering to Zion.
	Jeannette Morris, bore testimony to all
Sister Horne had said, “there is nothing
outside of the Gospel, God is no respector
of persons, the truth is free to all.” Isabel
Workman testified of being raised from al-
most death; when the consecrated oil

[Column 2]
touched her head, it was like electricity;
her son too had been raised from a very
low condition, spoke of the Word of
Wisdom and that the desire for tea had been
taken from her.
	Elmina S. Taylor, President of the
Y. L. M. I. A., said she had been blest in
attending the General Conference, referred
to evil speaking, especially against the
priesthood, knew our leaders were men of
God, and that we did not have to put out
our hands to steady the ark, said there
would be a greater apostacy than ever had
been.
	President Horne made closing remarks.
Sang “Lord dismiss us with thy blessing.”
Benediction by Julia C. Howe.
				M. L. MORRIS, Sec. pro-tem.


A TRIBUTE TO SISTER PETERSON

Dear Sister Wells:
	AGAIN I feel it my painful duty to say a
few words in regard to the departure of my
beloved friend and counselor, Sister S. A.
Peterson, so closely following our mutual
friend and co-laborer Sister M. W. Snow
whose death she mourned and wrote me her
deep sympathies for my bereavement.
Little did I then suppose she would so
soon leave me, to stand alone!
	Sister Peterson was true and faithful
under all circumstances and ever ready to
assist the poor and comfort the afflicted and
her loss will be felt by many. She was
devoted to her husband President C. Peter-
son and their numerous family who love and
reverence her memory, besides being a
public benefactor and visiting all the settle-
ments in the stake once or twice a year, feel-
ing an interest in every one’s welfare.
	I cannot express the desolation I felt in
being deprived of this my valued friend
and only remaining counselor. I feel to
acknowlege the hand of the Lord in this
His dispensation, for He doeth all things
well, "Blessed be the name of the Lord."
MARY A. P. HYDE.
May 27th, 1896.


U. W. P. C.

	THE Utah Woman’s Press Club met at
the office of the WOMAN'S EXPONENT,
Wednesday evening, June 3rd, 1896,
President E. B. Wells in the chair. Opened
with prayer by Mrs. C. C. R. Wells.
Minutes of previous meeting read and with
two exceptions approved. The exceptions
were that Miss Barton president of the Red
Cross society be mentioned by her full
name Clara Barton. Also that Mrs. Brown
who wrote the club, the interesting letter
from Washington D. C. be mentioned by
her full name Isabella Cameron Brown. A
visitor to the club for the evening was Miss
Sybil Stewart. Mrs. Emma J. McVicker
was unanimously voted a member of the
Club. Ruth M. Fox moved that Dr. R.
B. Pratt be chairman of a new program
committee and select her aids. Motion
carried. Dr. Pratt chose Dr. E. B. Fergu-
son and Phoebe C. Young as members of
the Committee.
	Phoebe C. Young read an interesting
paper on “The Armenians,” which will be
published.
	Remarks on the subject were made by
Mrs. E. B. Wells, Dr. Ferguson and others.
Meeting adjourned until June 30th, 8 p. m.
Closing prayer by Dr. R. B. Pratt.
L. S. WILCOX, Sec. pro tem.


Program for June 30th, 8 p. m,— Senti-
ments from Sir Walter Scott. Poem by
Dr. E. R. Shipp. Mrs. McVicker—verbal
account of visit to Denver. Mrs. Lizzie
Wilcox to read a chapter of Parliamentary
Law. Discussion.


MRS. HOWE TO YOUNG PEOPLE.

	AT a Young Peoples Meeting held
in Boston, Mrs. Julia Ward Howe, presi-
dent of the Association, occupied the chair
and made a graceful and appropriate ex-
tempore address. She said:
	“In introducing these young exponents
of our cause, I am acting as a sort of grand-
mother to the meeting. I hope you will
all agree with the little girl who said she
thought that grandmothers were a “good
plan.” It ought to be so, for one genera-
tion is lifted on the shoulders of the one
that goes before it. Mr. Bronson Alcott
once said, at a meeting, that he was weighed
down by the sins of the generations that
had gone before him, and I sprang up and
protested that I could not hear my ances-
tors thus maligned. I felt lifted up and in-
spired by the good deeds of my ancestors.
When girls first went to college they had to
endure dreadful prognostications by Presi-
dent Elliot and others. They were warned
of ‘downward influences’ and it was
feared that they would ‘lower the standard
of scholarship.’ These young men and wo-
men will show to-night how unnecessary
were these forebodings. We need their
presence. Hope springs eternal in the
hearts of youth. They are filled with the
splendid courage that conquers the world.
They bring back the freshness of our ideals,
grown dusty in our years of conflict. To-
night we shall gather great hope and en-
couragement from their voices.”


S. L. CO. W. S. A. 

	MINUTES of meeting held in fourteenth
Ward hall, April 21st. Sang “America."
Prayer by Mrs. McFarlane. Roll call.
Minutes of last meeting read and approved:
after which Miss Elliot, of New York,
spoke of her experiences in the East both
in politics and religion. Speaking of
politics, she said that it is the fad of the
hour in society in New York at the present
time. And Susan B. Anthony who was
received indifferently at first and later re-
ceived respectfully, was now sought after
by fashionable New York to speak at their
parties and dinners. She said that the eyes
of the whole world were on Utah and her
people at the present time: and that we
could not fully realize the advantage we
have over the women in the east. She
spoke at some length of the political situa-
tion in New York and of the thousands of
men of foreign birth who could not speak
the English language, who were allowed to
cast their vote and were willing to sell it
for twenty five cents while a woman of
education and superior intellect had no
voice in the matter. The average American
boy imbibes politics all his life while the
girls must be taught, and in no country in
the world do women have the opportunity
to teach them that the women of Utah
have. We must go ahead and interest
every women in Utah in the study of
political science. Benediction by Mrs.
Oswald.

> WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


[Column 1]
	ARMENIA.

	BY PHŒBE C. YOUNG.

	ARMENIA is,according to ancient tradition,
the original seat of one of the oldest civilized
people in the world; they claim
to be descended from Haik; whom they
say was the son of Tagarmah the son of
Gomer, the son of Japheth, the son of
Noah. He according to their tradition
established the Armenian kingdom in the
vicinity of Ararat the mountain on which
the Ark is said to have rested after the
flood; this was done at an uncertain date,
but is supposed to be about two thousand
years before the Christian Era.
	As a race they belong to the Indo
Germanic family of nations. The country
they possess is a high table land on the
southern slope of the Caucasus stretching
down towards Mesopotamia though its
boundaries have been changed many times
during the centuries of its history. Its
area is estimated at ninety thousand square
miles. Their oldest records contain noth-
ing beyond the facts that in ancient time
they were governed by independent kings
and afterwards became tributary to the
Assyrians and Medes,a fact that established
their antiquity as a nation in which it is
difficult to tell where myth ends and
history begins. We learn that about the
middle of the sixth century before Christ a
certain king Dikran of the Haiig dynasty
restored the independence of the kingdom,
the last king of this line was slain in battle
by Alexander the great, who conquered the
country and appointed Governors over it;
it is impossible in the space of one short
article to give even a faint description of the
changes and vicissitudes through which
Armenia passed through the next six hun-
dred years. Its subsequent history
exhibits an unbroken series of tumults and
wars; of violent successions to the throne;
despotic reigns and rapid decay. Subject
to the vigorous assaults of the Parthians
from the East and the more powerful
Romans from the West, “they sometimes
become partly dependent on one nation
and sometimes on the other. While in-
ternally the Nobles often broke through the
restraints of a feeble monarchy and claimed
the privileges of petty kings.”
	In six hundred thirty two the unhappy
country was subject to another form of
despotism under the Arabian Califs and
suffered terribly during their contest with
the Byzantine emperors. The rule of the
Sassanides was marked chiefly by their
sanguinary but unsuccessful attempts to
extirpate Christanity.
	The Byzantine Greeks on one side and
the Persians on the other seemed to regard
Armenia as their lawful or unlawful prey.
	In eight hundred and eighty five Aschod
of an old and powerful Armenian family
ascended the throne with the permission of
the Califs and founded the third Armenian
Dynasty; under this monarch and his suc- 
cessors Armenia was prosperous till the
eleventh century when divisions and inter-
nal strife began to weaken the country;
till at length the Greeks having murdered
the last monarch of the line seized part of the
kingdom, while the Turks and Kurds made
themselves masters of the rest.
	In thirteen hundred seventy four Asia
Minor was conquered by the Egyptian Sul-
tan Schalean, since that time Armenia has
remained subject to the despotism of the
Turks and Persians with the exception of

[Column 2]
the parts won from Persia in the present
century by Russia, and which are better
governed.
	Notwithstanding the political storms and
the persecutions passed through by this
much enduring people they have steadily
persevered their nationality both in its
physical and moral lineaments. They
have preserved their faith, although and
through persecution,oppression and sorrow;
they only possess to day a relic of their
ancient culture; they can still boast a higher
civilization than their conquerors. In con-
sequence of the great disturbances and
religious persecutions of the middle ages,
many of the Armenians sought refuge in
other countries and we find them scattered
over all Asia and Europe; in Hungary, in
Transylvania and Galicia they number over
ten thousand and they are very numerous
in Russia, but most in Asia Minor, in that
neighborhood alone they number over two
hundred thousand. The north east portion
of Armenia, about one third of the whole
was wrested from Persia by Russia in 1828
and is under the rule of the Czar which is to
be preferred to that of the Turk. About
a sixth part to the south east still belongs to
Persia, two thirds of the Armenian area is
Turkish.
	After the war of 1877—78 between
Russia and Turkey the Berlin conference
sanctioned the session of a strip of Armenia
to Russia, and the Sultan of Turkey under-
took to guarantee the Armenians security
against the Circassians and Kurds, also to
report to the European powers the measures
taken for much needed reforms. It is need-
less to say that this promise has been broken
in every particular.
	Christianity was introduced into Armenia
as early as the second century, but not
firmly established till about the third
century. It afterwards became the centre
of a great controversy regarding the two-
fold nature of Christ. Numbers of learned
men flocked to their colleges because of the
spirit of scientific inquiry that early mani-
fested itself among them, to a far greater
extent than in any other of the Eastern
Churches. The Bible was translated into
the Armenian language early in the fifth
century which was no doubt the reason of
the religious fervor that prevailed.
	Great efforts have been made, and great
influence has been brought at different
times to bear, to induce this people to ac-
knowledge the supremacy and infallibility
of the Popes of Rome, such efforts have
always failed, for notwithstanding all
changes the masses of the people have re-
mained true to their own peculiar opinions.
The Armenian Church entertains peculiar
ideas with regard to what is called the
seven sacraments. “They believe that at
baptism one must be sprinkled three times
and as often immersed. That confirmation
must be conjoined with baptism; that the
Lord’s supper must be celebrated with pure
wine and leavened bread, that the bread
must be dipped in the wine before being
handed round; that extreme unction is to
be administered to the Priests alone, and
that not before but after death. It also be-
lieves in the worship of Saints but not in
the doctrine of purgatory; secular Priests
must marry once, but none are at liberty to
marry a second time.”
	As a race the Armenians are remarkable
for their physical beauty and intelligence,
this last is noticable both in their literature
and their great capacity for business; in fact,

[Column 3]
this is so well known that they are employed
by all the leading business houses of Con-
stantinople, whenever men are needed for
positions of trust and dexterity in business.
It has been remarked that however much
the Armenians may be scattered in what-
ever countries they may be found; the
printing office is always a leading feature in
their colonies.
	The Armenian women as a rule are fine-
looking with intelligent faces and womanly
bearing. It is a well known fact that
among the Oriental races the women as they
advance in age become decrepit and lose
every vestige of beauty before we should
consider they had arrived at middle age.
	This is not the case with Armenian wo-
men as they increase in years their intel-
ligence and good looks become more mani-
fest. The history of the Armenians during
the last twenty years has been a series of
suffering,oppression and misrule; subject to
a nation “whose government is said to be a
praise to evil doers and a terror to those
who do well.”
	For notwithstanding the promise of the
Sultan at the treaty of Berlin to prevent
and punish the depredation of the Kurds
and to rectify the abuses of the Government
their condition far from growing better has
been growing steadily worse. We are
naturally led to inquire the causes that have
brought about the existing state of events
that are occuring in Armenia. One of
these reasons seems to be the greater educa-
tion and growing sentiment of independence
and nationality among the Armenians
themselves and “though they desire to lead
a peaceful life, peace is something they are
forbidden to enjoy.”
	[To be Continued.]


R. S. & P. A. REPORTS.

	THE Primary Conference of Bear Lake
Stake convened at St. Charles, May 30,
1896, President Annie Laker presiding.
Verbal reports of the different associations
throughout the stake were listened to with
great interest, showing that the work is in
a prosperous condition. After which an
interesting program was rendered by the
children. The general instructions were
good. Large attendance present. 
	NORA PUGMIRE, Sec.

BBAR LAKE STAKE.
	THE Bear Lake Stake Relief Socitey
convened in the St. Charles meeting house,
May 30th, 1896. Of the Stake presidency
there were Brother William L. Rich and
J. W. Stucki present. President Linsday
presided: after the usual opening exercises
and the roll was called. She made a few
opening remarks in which she said the ob-
ject of the conferences was to hear from the
presidents, the condition of their associa-
tion and also to be enabled to give such
counsel as our Heavenly Father and the
presiding sisters desired to impart, for the
mutual good and benefit of the sisters in
Zion. She trusted we might hear such
things as would cause us to return to our
homes with renewed energy and strength to
perform our duties.
	The following associations were then re-
ported as in good condition. 
	St. Charles was reported by President
Galloway. Fish Haven, President Howell;
Garden City, Counselor A. Cook; Wood-


ruff, President Sessions; Bloomington,
Counselor Patterson; Wardburra, President
Dalrymple; Dingle, Sister Jane Sparks;
Montpelier, Sister [-]. P. Linsday; Benning-
ton, President Vanorman, Georgetown,
Sister Bee; Thomas Fork, Counselor
Cook.
	Counselor L. M. Hart spoke of the duties
of the members of the Relief Society, urged
the sisters to gain a testimony of the truth
of the great work, for the time is coming
when those only who have this testimony
will be enabled to stand the trials that are
coming upon this people, spoke also of the
necessity of educating the young in the
principles of the Gospel. Brother J. W.
Stucki gave the sisters encouragment,
spoke of the aid they were to the Bishops
of the different wards, not only in caring for
the poor and sick, but in their willingness
to donate to all charitable purposes. Spoke
of the advantages of the young people,
much would be expected of the present
generation.
	An Organ Solo was then rendered by
Mr. A. Osband. President Laker of the
Primary spoke of the strong bond of friend-
ship that existed between the sisters who
were laboring together in the different as-
sociations. Meeting adjourned by singing
"Praise The Lord." Benediction Elder
Laker.
	At 2 p. m. The choir sang, "O Lord
our help." Prayer b[-] Brother J. Stucki
Singing, "My Lord the light of all my
Life." While the sacrament was ad-
ministered the choir sang sacramental hymn.
Several associations were then reported all
of which were doing a good work.
	President Lindsay was overjoyed to see
so many out considering the rainy weather
and very bad roads. She felt satisfied with
the improvement that was being made.
The Saints had much to be thankful for.
Spoke of the judgments of God that would
come upon the wicked; urged the Saints to
live in such a way that God would see fit to
preserve us from these judgments, said we
should awake to a sense of our duties, in-
voked the blessing of God upon all.
President Nancy Pugmire, of the Young
Ladies Associations felt that the remarks
that had been made were good and timely,
spoke at some length on the needs of do-
mestic economy, this is something that is
neglected too much in the homes of the
Latter day Saints. President W. L. Rich
endorsed the remarks of the previous
speakers, thought if the speakers depended
on inspiration they never sit down without
saying something that would be beneficial
to some one. The minutes of the meeting
were read and received, after which confer-
ence was adjourned by the choir singing an
anthem. ‘‘They that trust in the Lord.”
Benediction by President W. L. Rich.
ROSE HILLIER, Sec.

[Column 1]
IN MEMORIAM.

	DIED at her home in Manti, of old age, on
March 4th, 1896.
	Emeline Whiting Cox. Deceased was the
daughter of Elisha Whiting and Sally Hewlitt
who were natives of Vermont, but removed to
Nelson, Portage Co. Ohio, in the early settlement
of that state where the subject of this sketch was
born July 23, 1817
	In 1835 she was married to Frederick Walter
Cox. Sometime after she and her husband em-
braced the Gospel and removed to Far West, Mo.
They endured all the persecutions, hardships and
privations to which the Saints were subjected dur-
ing their residence in that state.
	Brother Cox, being a man of great faith and

[Column 2]
marked ability, was frequently called to perform
missions in different parts of the United States,
consequently his wife was often left to care for
her little ones alone, but as she was a woman of
rare executive ability, and by nature specially
adapted to the care of children, she performed
these duties wisely and well.
	After being driven from Missouri, they settled
in Lima where they remained for nearly six years,
but were finally driven from their home which to-
gether with their stacks of grain out buildings
&c, were burned to the ground before their eyes,
leaving the family almost penniless but glad to
escape with their lives.
	They next settled in Nauvoo, but before a year
had passed were again compelled to move, this
time they took up their line of march towards the
setting sun.
	At the next halting place Mt. Pisgah, Sister
Cox had more than her full share of the sickness
and sorrow which seemed to be the portion of the
Saints during their soiourn at that place.
	Her beloved father and mother and two of her
own little daughters all within the space of a few
weeks followed each other to the grave and while
she was sick—indeed so low that her life was
despaired of, Br. Cox was making the coffins for
these little ones and bearing them in his own
arms to the grave.
	They afterwards removed to Council Bluffs—
where they were called to part with another
child, this time their infant boy.
	In the spring of 1852 Brother Cox and his
family crossed the Missouri River and began their
long wearisome journey across the plains.
	On the banks of the Platte River another little
daughter was born, who lived and prospered in
spite of the unfavorable circumstances attending
her advent.
	On the 4th of October 1852, Sister Cox once
more found a resting place in Manti, where she
has since resided ever helping in a heroic manner
to bear the hardships and burdens incident to a
pioneer life in a hostile Indian country.
	In the spring of 1863 Brother Cox was called to
take a mission to England, in August her last
child a little girl was born, and eighteen months
later Sister Cox was afflicted with a cancer, the
removal of which almost cost her, her life and
the following summer when the baby was two
years old she was called from earth thus adding
another grievous trial which was greatly aggra-
vated by the absence of the husband and father.
Sister Cox was an exemplary wife and mother a
faithful friend, and a conscientious Latter-day
Saint.
	She was a woman of intelligence, and refine-
ment, an agreeable companion with a keen sense
of humor and an inexhaustible fund of anecdote
and reminiscenses of her early life.
	She was the mother of twelve children—eight
of whom survive her—their respect and love for
her was unbounded and of her it can be truly
said “Her children rise up and call her blessed”—
and also that she has fought the good fight, has
kept the faith and has gone to receive the reward
which her long years of trial, toil and sacrifice
entitle her to; her remains were tenderly borne to
their last resting place by her grandsons who hold
her memory in grateful remembrance.
	After life’s fitful fever she sleeps well.
COM.

RESOLUTIONS OF RESPECT.
	Resolutions of respect adopted by the W. H.
P. R. Class in memory of Sister Rachel Ann
Gatherum, the beloved wife of John Gatherum
who was born in Heber City, Wasatch Co., May
29th 1865 and departed this life April 20th, 1896.
	Whereas our Heavenly Father has called from
our midst one of our faithful members, we desire
to offer our sympathies and condolence to the hus-
band and friends of deceased, for she was a duti-
ful daughter, a devoted wife, and a loving
mother, ever seeking to implant faith and purity
in the hearts of her children.
	Resolved that we, the officers a[-]d members of
our association do recognize in her death the loss
of an energetic co-laborer, whose example was
ever worthy of emulation.
	Resolved that these resolutions be placed upon
the records of our association and a copy be sent
to the husband and family.

His home she enters there to be a light
Shining within when all without is night
A guardian angel o’er his life presiding
Doubling his pleasures and all his cares dividing.

Winning him back when mingling in the throng
From a vain world we love alas too long

[Column 3]
To fireside happiness and hours of ease
Blessed with that charm the certainty to please.

How oft her eyes read his, her gentle mind
To all his wishes all his thoughts inclined,
Still subject, ever on the watch to borrow
Mirth of his mirth, sorrow of his sorrow.

EMILY G. CLUFF, President.
SARAH L. DIXON, Coun.
DELIA I. BOOTH,
OLIVE BEAN,
H. A. KELLER.
}Committee.
["}Committee." is written to the left of the three lines above it]

	REBECCA HEATON NEBEKER, wife of Henry
Nebeker, and daughter of William and Frances
Heaton departed this life April 24, 1896, at Sigurd
Sevier Co, Utah. She was born Feb. 28, 1834, at
Little Orton, Yorkshire England. She joined the
Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints in
her native land; emigrated to Utan in 1862. In
1865 she married Henry Nebeker at Payson, Utah
Co. One year later they were called to help set-
tle the Muddy Mission, where they remained un-
til the mission was broken up in 1870. In that
year they removed to Glenwood, Sevier Co. where
they remained four years. In that Ward she held
the office of Counselor to the President of the
Reliet Society.
	In the winter of 1874 she came to Sigurd form-
erly part of Vermillion Ward, where she has
since resided. She was chosen President of the
Relief Society of the Vermillion Ward Feb. 17,
1887, which position she filled with honor until
the division of the Ward in 1895. At that time
she was called to the presidency in the Sigurd
Society which position she held at the time of her
death. She had also been a Sunday School
teacher for many years, and was prominent in
public circles. Was ever ready to minister to the
poor and needy; had ever a comforting word for
the distressed. The cause of death was quick
consumption. She was the mother of seven
children, six of whom are still living. She had
eleven grand-children and one great-grand-child.
She died surrounded by her children, brother,
sisters and other relatives and leaves behind a
numerous host of friends to mourn her departure.
She died faithful to the Gospel and in the hope of
a glorious resurrection.
PAULINE LARSON DASTRUP.
Sigurd, Sevier Co. Utah. May 19, 1896.

	IN memory of Sister Ovilla Levitt Brown who
died April 20th 1896, aged sixty seven years one
month and nine days, and daughter of John and
Lucy Levitt, she was born in Habley Co. Sher-
brook Canada and removed with her parents
when nine years old to Kirtland Ohio. Married
Brother Philander Brown, Oct. 13, 1848, at Cam-
bray, Hillside Co. Michigan and was baptized at
Council Bluffs in 1858, by Elder William Folsom,
resided in Farmington, Davis Co., went to the
Muddy with her husband on a mission and moved
to Provo in 1873.
	Whereas it has pleased our Heavenly Father
to take our beloved Sister to a higher sphere,
therefore be it,
	Resolved, that we her co-laborers members of
the Second Ward Relief Society of Provo cherish
her memory and emulate her virtues energy and
perseverance. She was a useful woman in the
community among her sisters in sickness, and
faithful and true in the higher order of marriage
and was of a kind and humane disposition.
	She was a teacher and appraiser in our Society
for many years, and a counselor for eight years in
the Primary Association when organized in 1878.
We sympathise with the family in their bereave-
ment and desire to express our condolence.
	Resolved that these resolutions be placed upon
the records of these Societies and published in the
WOMAN'S EXPONENT.
SARAH SCOTT, Prest.
SARAH GOODMAN, Coun.
M. A. TILL,
MARTHA HARRIS.

uals, but it is going to help Miss Couzins,
whose active career has been interesting in
showing woman's capacity for executive

[Column 1]
CONFERENCE OF THE N. A. W. S. A.

(Continued)
REMARKS BY MRS. ELECTA BULLOCK OF
UTAH. 
Ladies and Gentlemen:
	I am very glad the Chairman said some-
thing about applause. I am not a public
speaker and unless I hear a little enthusiasm
from the audience I do not believe I can say
a word. (Applause) It appears to me
that you are all asleep when we are hear-
ing the grandest sentiments that ever ema-
nated from anyone’s mouth. Mrs. Wells
says we must hurry she wants to hear
from those eminent speakers—so do we, so
I will just say a word or two—I don’t
want to leave out beautiful Utah County
which is considered the garden spot of the
Territory. I am proud of it and we have
a great many very good suffragists there
and they had occasion to display their true
colors in the late conflict in the Constitu-
tional Convention. In many instances
there were men that I thought I could stake
my life on their support and when we pre-
sented the petition to them to sign, some of
those leading gentlemen said in reply to
my remark that I would like to see their
names head the list, “O yes, we believe in
equal suffrage but you see it I should put
my name to that petition—why you see it
would defeat us.”There it was again,
willing to sell the liberty of one-half the
people of this Territory for Statehood.
(Applause.) That is the way we have been
treated. It was the old spirit but I am
very happy to say it is giving way to the
new conditions that surround us—I must
not forget I told Mrs. Wells I would only
say a word or two, but I cannot stop until
I say a word in regard to those honorable
delegates to the Constitutional Convention
from Utah County—they were earnest 
workers and I heard twelve of them were
united and they were heroes in our cause.
(Applause.) Although we have been en-
slaved so long and even at the risk of de-
feating statehood, such men as S. R. Thur-
man and F. S. Richards of this County and
others true to their party platform (Mrs.
Wells interrupting Mrs. Bullock. Excuse
me Mrs. Wells)—stood up and said “If we
can’t have Statehood without the liberty of
all the people we don’t want it” (applause)
I say that is true patriotism for you gentle- 
men and ladies—that is the kind of patriot-
ism that gained our independence. Thank-
ing you for your attention. (Applause.)
	Miss Anthony:—Mrs. Wells, who is the 
General on this occasion says I must say
something, Now it seems to me in view of
what I feel that you all feel to be a fact that
there is no doubt with regard to the adop-
tion of the Constitution by a majority of
the electors of this Territory, and that after
it has been adopted by the votes that there
is no doubt whatever that Congress will ad-
mit the State into the Union, the Territory
into the Union as a State, it seems to me
that the one word that I want to say to you
is as if you were already a State, as if the
women of the State were already enfran-
chised. It seems to me that the work for
the women of Utah is exactly like that of
the women of Colorado and that is that they
shall commence the study of the great
principles that underlie our government—
the principles of civil government and the
principles of municipal government and
State and National governments, and that

[Column 2]
the objects and aims of our societies which
have hitherto been demanding and urging
the enfranchisement of your women, that
you shall make them now preparatory
schools or schools to prepare the women and
the men of your respective communities to
vote intelligently. This cannot be done
except by good and earnest study. Men
have not studied the principles of their
government. If they had they would have
enfranchised the women long ago, and if
the women had stood by the great work of
studying the laws and principles their de-
sires would have been accomplished long
ago. The men of today have not sufficient-
ly studied the rise and fall of the political
parties of this nation—a majority of the
men know nothing about the Constitution
of the State or of the nation or the work of
the women. Women should not only be
intelligent upon all questions of govern-
ment but they should educate their sons,
their brothers their fathers and their hus-
bands. They should invite them to their
meetings with them and together study
those questions. The introduction of wo-
men into government affairs will result in
higher intelligence among men as well as
among women. It seems to me the result
will not be at all what is intended if we do
not have greater intelligence everywhere.
	Now my good friends, it is supposed by
some that we are under pay from the
National Association. I want to disabuse
your minds—we are under pay from no-
body, we are, as the woman says, making
this trip free gratis and Mrs. Wells has as-
sumed all the financial responsibilities of
our stay here and of course the expenses
are not small. Utah has always done her
share towards helping to forward the cause
of woman. As I told you last night, five
states have passed a resolution submitting
the proposition to be voted upon not as a
part of the constitution as in your State, but
to be submitted as a separate measure.
When that question is submitted to the
people of this Territory every individual
man and woman must work, for it requires
immense educational work to reach out to
every one of the precincts of the State to
educate each individual to vote intelligently
(To the girls taking up the collection—
“Girls, don’t go too fast, give everybody a
chance to get their pocket books out.”)
	Now my friends, in regard to our Suf-
frage Association, we have thirty-six al-
ready organized, and at Atlanta last winter
there was proposed an organization Com-
mittee and the sum of $1800.00 was raised
right on the spot in order to carry the good
work into the various states. Utah for the
last twenty years has been one of the best
organized states. Utah and New York
have run a tilt as to which should be the
banner Suffrage Society in the nation.
Sometimes Utah has come with a much
larger delegation and New York has come
second, but I believe this year that New
York stands as the banner state of the
whole thirty-six as to the largest member-
ship. You know the rule is that you pay
into your State society twenty-five cents for
every local member and then your State
society sends ten cents out of every one of
those twenty-five cents to the treasurer of
your National Association so that is the
way we find out which State is the best or-
ganized, which state has the largest number
of members by the amount of money paid
in. New York this year sent in $165.00
to the treasurer that you know shows that

[Column 3] 
she has a membership of 1650 for which
the ten cents fund has been paid into the
National treasury. I forgot the exact
amount Utah sent [Mrs. Wells, $115.00 and
we have hundreds of women who have not
paid their dues] Mrs. Wells says  $115.00 and
there are hundreds and hundreds of women
in Utah this year and men too who have
not paid their little dues into the local
society, and when you do not pay into the
local society the State society is unable to
pay its ten cents to the National Society.
So you see when you join a local society
and pay your $1.00 membership fee which
I think is your membership fee, or 50 cents
or whatever it is, each man and each wo-
man that pays that membership fee into the
local society has contributed ten cents of what-
ever the amount may be towards helping
on with the good work. In sustaining
your local society you are sustaining
financially your State and National As-
sociation as well. That is what we all do.
We want to be one grand solid army from
one end of the nation to the other each
local society is auxiliary to the State society
and each State society is auxiliary to the
National Association, and in this way we
shall become solidly organized and when-
ever there is any great crisis coming in the
state or in the nation the women can band
themselves together to help carry the
measure if it is a good one or to defeat it if
the reverse. I do not believe that you wo-
men with the ballot in your hands will
make this purely partisan and vote for an
unworthy man because he is of that
political party, but on the other hand wo-
men will do in Utah as they have done in
Wyoming and Colorado and as they have
done in the State of Kansas in school elec-
tions that [---] hold themselves as a
combined power to vote for the best man
that is nominated, for the best party that is
in the field.
